text: Hello, I am Dr. Sanjay Mandal, GI surgeon from Kolkata and today I will be showing a video of laparoscopic cholecystectomy in a patient of situs inversus. This patient was a sixty six year old lady who had presented with pain in the left upper quadrant of the abdomen and on investigation it was revealed that she had situs inversus with a thick walled gallbladder with multiple stones. The surgery was planned with a modified OT setup, the patient was kept legs apart, the surgeon standing in between the legs, the camera holder on the right side and the assistant on the left side with the monitor towards the patient's left shoulder. The port positions were also modified and you can see the diagram depicting them. Now once we enter the abdomen you can see the altered anatomy with the liver on the left side, the fundus of the stomach on the right and the pylorus of the stomach on the left side. The first working port is the five millimeter epigastric port to ascertain the position of the gallbladder. The next working ports all on the left side below the left costal margin are in the mid clavicular line and anterior axillary line.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to expose the gallbladder, carefully dissecting through significant pericholecystic adhesions with visible inflammation. The altered anatomy due to situs inversus is evident, with the liver positioned on the left side. All working ports are five millimeters, allowing flexibility for conversion to ten millimeters for clipping the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to separate the duodenum from the Hartmann's pouch of the gallbladder, exposing the Calot's triangle. Flimsy adhesions are carefully removed using hook diathermy, revealing the cystic duct and artery. The mid-clavicular port is utilized as the main working port, which will be converted to a ten millimeter port for clipping the artery and duct.

The surgeon performs sharp and blunt dissection of the Calot's triangle to expose the cystic duct and cystic artery. Blunt dissection using a suction cannula is employed to manage acute inflammation and edema. A spurting vessel is controlled with monopolar or bipolar diathermy. A window is created in the posterior Calot's triangle, allowing for further dissection and identification of the cystic artery. The mid clavicular port is converted from five millimeters to ten millimeters, and the cystic artery is clipped and cut.

The surgeon performs further dissection to achieve the critical view of safety, milking the cystic duct to move any stones upwards. Staggered clips are placed on the wide cystic duct, which is then transected. The gallbladder is dissected off the liver using diathermy.

The surgeon retrieves a single fallen stone using a scoop. After dissecting the gallbladder from the liver, the surgical field is thoroughly washed with normal saline to remove spilled bile, clots, and blood. The clips are checked, and a specimen retrieval bag is introduced to extract the gallbladder. A drain is placed in the subhepatic space, and the specimen is extracted, completing the surgery.
